title: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.7 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.7 (Conditions for consent) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.5: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.5' running on sourcetype access_combined and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=web` `sourcetype="access_combined"` (clientip, uri, method, status, useragent) for consent page interactions; or custom HEC JSON events with explicit consent fields
app: Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (Splunkbase 3186), HTTP Event Collector (HEC — platform capability for structured JSON from consent APIs)
spl:
index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d
    (uri="*/consent*" OR uri="*/privacy-preferences*")
| rex field=uri_query "action=(?<consent_action>[^&]+)"
| eval consent_event=coalesce(consent_action, if(status=200, "page_view", "error"))
| stats count by clientip, uri, consent_event, status
| sort - count
description: Preserves a searchable trail of consent grant, refuse, and withdraw events from web applications for accountability and consent withdrawal parity requirements.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes web and sourcetypes access_combined. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ingest Apache/nginx access logs via TA 3186 or Universal Forwarder file inputs; (2) for richer evidence, emit HEC JSON from the consent microservice with explicit `action`, `purpose_id`, and hashed subject ID fields; (3) map URIs to consent purposes via a lookup `consent_uri_map.csv`; (4) restrict index ACLs to privacy teams; (5) schedule weekly reporting for consent withdrawal ratio monitoring. Indexes required: `index=web`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=access_combined`.
visualization: Time chart (consent page hits), Stacked bar (grant vs revoke/withdraw), Table (top consent events by URI). Key fields: `clientip`, `consent_event`, `status`, `uri`.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server

knownFalsePositives: Consent and preference events from product tests, training sandboxes, or staged marketing launches that are already approved.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (Splunkbase 3186), HTTP Event Collector (HEC — platform capability for structured JSON from consent APIs).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=web` `sourcetype="access_combined"` (clientip, uri, method, status, useragent) for consent page interactions; or custom HEC JSON events with explicit consent fields.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest Apache/nginx access logs via TA 3186 or Universal Forwarder file inputs; (2) for richer evidence, emit HEC JSON from the consent microservice with explicit `action`, `purpose_id`, and hashed subject ID fields; (3) map URIs to consent purposes via a lookup `consent_uri_map.csv`; (4) restrict index ACLs to privacy teams; (5) schedule weekly reporting for consent withdrawal ratio monitoring.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d
    (uri="*/consent*" OR uri="*/privacy-preferences*")
| rex field=uri_query "action=(?<consent_action>[^&]+)"
| eval consent_event=coalesce(consent_action, if(status=200, "page_view", "error"))
| stats count by clientip, uri, consent_event, status
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail (Art. 7)** — Preserves a searchable trail of consent grant, refuse, and withdraw events from web applications for accountability and consent withdrawal parity requirements.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=web` `sourcetype="access_combined"` (clientip, uri, method, status, useragent) for consent page interactions; or custom HEC JSON events with explicit consent fields. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (Splunkbase 3186), HTTP Event Collector (HEC — platform capability for structured JSON from consent APIs). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: web; **sourcetype**: access_combined. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=web, sourcetype="access_combined", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **consent_event** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by clientip, uri, consent_event, status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (consent page hits), Stacked bar (grant vs revoke/withdraw), Table (top consent events by URI).
grandmaExplanation: We preserves a searchable trail of consent grant, refuse, and withdraw events from web applications for accountability and consent withdrawal parity requirements so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
